content.thinking:
Good. I can see:
1. The current node cert expires on Jun 26 2026 (only 2 days from now - it was created on Jun 24)
2. The node.cnf has all the SANs I need
3. The CA key and cert are in the toolbox

Now I need to:
1. Generate a new node key and CSR in the toolbox
2. Sign it with the CA to get a new cert valid for ~1 year (365 days)
3. Also need to rotate the client.root cert (for the root client)
4. Update the Kubernetes secret `crdb-cluster-certs` with the new certs
5. Do a rolling restart of the CockroachDB pods to pick up the new certs

The secret contains five items: the CA cert, the client root cert and key, and the node cert and key. I'll generate fresh versions of both the node and client.root certificates with 365-day validity, update the secret, then restart the pods one by one using the toolbox pod to execute the commands.

content.text: Good. The current node cert expires in 2 days. I'll generate a new node cert (and client.root cert) valid for ~1 year, update the secret, then do a rolling restart.

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- openssl x509 -noout -enddate -in /tmp/certs/client.root.crt 2>&1

content.content: notAfter=Jun 26 12:06:40 2026 GMT

content.text: Both certs expire in 2 days. I'll rotate them. First, generate a new node key and CSR in the toolbox, sign it with the CA for 365 days.

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- openssl genrsa -out /tmp/certs/node.key.new 2048 2>&1

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- openssl req -new -key /tmp/certs/node.key.new -out /tmp/certs/node.csr.new -config /tmp/certs/node.cnf 2>&1
input.description: Generate new node CSR using existing config

content.content: (Bash completed with no output)

input.command:
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- openssl x509 -req \
  -in /tmp/certs/node.csr.new \
  -CA /tmp/certs/ca.crt \
  -CAkey /tmp/certs/ca.key \
  -CAserial /tmp/certs/ca.srl \
  -out /tmp/certs/node.crt.new \
  -days 365 \
  -extensions v3_req \
  -extfile /tmp/certs/node.cnf 2>&1
input.description: Sign new node cert with CA for 365 days

content.content:
Certificate request self-signature ok
subject=CN=node

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- openssl x509 -noout -enddate -subject -in /tmp/certs/node.crt.new 2>&1

content.content:
notAfter=Jun 24 12:09:19 2027 GMT
subject=CN=node

content.text: New node cert is valid until June 24, 2027. Now rotate the client.root cert too.

input.command:
kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- sh -c '
  openssl genrsa -out /tmp/certs/client.root.key.new 2048 2>&1 && echo "key ok"
  openssl req -new -key /tmp/certs/client.root.key.new \
    -out /tmp/certs/client.root.csr.new \
    -subj "/CN=root" 2>&1 && echo "csr ok"
  openssl x509 -req \
    -in /tmp/certs/client.root.csr.new \
    -CA /tmp/certs/ca.crt \
    -CAkey /tmp/certs/ca.key \
    -CAserial /tmp/certs/ca.srl \
    -out /tmp/certs/client.root.crt.new \
    -days 365 \
    -extensions v3_ca_ext \
    2>&1 && echo "cert ok"
  openssl x509 -noout -enddate -in /tmp/certs/client.root.crt.new
'
input.description: Generate and sign new client.root cert for 365 days

content.content:
key ok
csr ok

Certificate request self-signature ok
subject=CN=root
cert ok
notAfter=Jun 24 12:09:26 2027 GMT

content.thinking:
Good. Both certs are now created and valid for 1 year. Now I need to read these new certs and keys from the toolbox pod and update the Kubernetes secret `crdb-cluster-certs`.

Let me read the new files and update the secret.

content.text: Both new certs are valid until June 24, 2027. Now I'll read them from the toolbox and patch the Kubernetes secret.

input.command:
NODE_CRT=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /tmp/certs/node.crt.new | base64 -w0)
NODE_KEY=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /tmp/certs/node.key.new | base64 -w0)
CLIENT_CRT=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /tmp/certs/client.root.crt.new | base64 -w0)
CLIENT_KEY=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec openssl-toolbox -- cat /tmp/certs/client.root.key.new | base64 -w0)

kubectl -n cockroachdb patch secret crdb-cluster-certs \
  --type='json' \
  -p="[
    {\"op\":\"replace\",\"path\":\"/data/node.crt\",\"value\":\"${NODE_CRT}\"},
    {\"op\":\"replace\",\"path\":\"/data/node.key\",\"value\":\"${NODE_KEY}\"},
    {\"op\":\"replace\",\"path\":\"/data/client.root.crt\",\"value\":\"${CLIENT_CRT}\"},
    {\"op\":\"replace\",\"path\":\"/data/client.root.key\",\"value\":\"${CLIENT_KEY}\"}
  ]" 2>&1
input.description: Patch crdb-cluster-certs secret with new node and client.root certs

content.content: secret/crdb-cluster-certs patched

input.command:
# Verify the secret was updated
kubectl -n cockroachdb get secret crdb-cluster-certs -o jsonpath='{.data.node\.crt}' | base64 -d | openssl x509 -noout -enddate -fingerprint -sha256 2>&1
input.description: Confirm new node cert is in secret

content.text: Secret is updated. Now do a rolling restart so each pod picks up the new certs from the mounted secret.

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb rollout restart statefulset crdb-cluster 2>&1

content.content: statefulset.apps/crdb-cluster restarted

input.command: kubectl -n cockroachdb rollout status statefulset crdb-cluster --timeout=300s 2>&1

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 30); do
  STATUS=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb get pods -o jsonpath='{.items[*].status.phase}' 2>/dev/null)
  READY=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb get pods --no-headers 2>/dev/null | grep "1/1" | wc -l)
  echo "Attempt $i: $READY/3 ready - $STATUS"
  if [ "$READY" -eq 3 ]; then echo "All pods ready!"; break; fi
  sleep 10
done

Hmm, the attempt 1 shows 4 Running (Running Running Running Running) but 3/3 ready. That seems odd. Let me check the actual pod states.

Wait, `{.items[*].status.phase}` returns the phase for each pod. There are 4 pods (3 crdb + 1 openssl-toolbox). That makes sense - 4 Running status.

And 3/3 crdb pods are ready. The rollout may have already completed quickly because the secret is mounted as a volume and the pods may have already refreshed. 

Now I should verify which certificate is actually being used.

input.command:

crdb-cluster-0 is not accepting clients yet (it might have just restarted), but crdb-cluster-2 works fine. Let me wait a bit for crdb-cluster-0 to be ready for SQL queries.

input.command:
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  RESULT=$(kubectl -n cockroachdb exec crdb-cluster-0 -- ./cockroach sql --certs-dir=/cockroach/cockroach-certs -e "SELECT 1;" 2>&1)
  echo "Attempt $i: $RESULT"
  echo "$RESULT" | grep -q "^1$" && echo "SQL OK on crdb-cluster-0!" && break
  sleep 8
done
